mnemonics: Async State Initialization Pattern: 'A Single Source of Truth Initiates Properly'
Remember the pattern: Fetch Data -> Set State -> Render UI
Think of useEffect as the 'componentDidMount for data-fetching'
keyPoints: Async operations (like data fetching) cannot be done directly in the render function or at the top level of a functional component.
The useEffect hook is the primary tool for performing side effects, including async state initialization, after the component has rendered.
You must provide a dependency array to useEffect to control when the effect runs (e.g., an empty array `[]` to run only once on mount).
Always handle the loading and error states in your component to provide a good user experience.
Cleanup is crucial to prevent memory leaks, especially for operations that might complete after a component has unmounted (e.g., cancelling a fetch).
commonMistakes: Placing the async fetch call directly in the component's body, causing infinite re-renders.
Forgetting to handle the loading state, resulting in trying to render data that doesn't exist yet.
Not handling errors from the async operation, causing the app to break silently.
Omitting the dependency array in useEffect, causing the effect to run after every single render.
Not cleaning up ongoing async operations (e.g., with AbortController) when the component unmounts, leading to 'can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' warnings.
